tables.title: Pediatric Bradycardia Management Summary
rows: Perfusion status matters more than the number alone
Hypoxia is the most common cause of pediatric bradycardia
HR < 60 with poor perfusion despite adequate O2/ventilation
Rapid push; same concentration as cardiac arrest
Only for vagal tone or primary AV block; not for hypoxic bradycardia
When bradycardia refractory to O2, compressions, and meds
Requires sedation if conscious; temporary bridge
More reliable capture; requires cardiology/ICU expertise
Hypoxia, acidosis, electrolytes, hypothermia, drugs, ICP
Must be treated concurrently — not just the heart rate

tables.title: Atropine Quick-Reference for Pediatric Bradycardia
rows: Increased vagal tone, primary AV block

tables.title: Sinus Bradycardia vs Other Bradyarrhythmias
rows: May be normal (1st degree), dropped (2nd), or independent (3rd)
Variable; ventricular rate slow in high-grade block
May be irregular (2nd degree) or regular but dissociated (3rd)
Congenital heart disease, myocarditis, drug toxicity
May improve 1st/2nd degree; usually ineffective in 3rd degree
